Starting from the basic observation that some things move and others rest, he identifies rotational motion: a spinning globe whose centre is at rest while its circumference revolves. A single rotation distributes proportional speeds to larger and smaller circles -- a remarkable fact that sets up the taxonomy of motion to follow.
summary: The Athenian solemnly invokes the gods to assist in proving their own existence, then begins his systematic analysis of motion. He starts with the basic distinction between rest and motion, then examines rotational motion -- where a spinning object is both at rest (at its centre) and in motion (at its circumference), with proportional speeds distributed to circles of different sizes.
distilled: The proof begins with a taxonomy of motion, starting from the observation that rotational motion unites rest and motion in a single object, distributing proportional speeds to its larger and smaller circles.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The invocation of the gods to assist in proving their own existence is a moment of high philosophical drama -- and deliberate irony. The "rope" metaphor continues the river-crossing imagery from 10.19. The analysis of rotational motion begins the ten-fold classification of types of motion (completed in 10.21-22) that culminates in the identification of self-motion as the primary type. The observation about proportional speeds in concentric circles reflects contemporary Greek astronomical knowledge -- Eudoxus of Cnidus (a contemporary of Plato at the Academy) developed a system of concentric rotating spheres to explain planetary motion. Cross-reference: Timaeus 34a describes the World Soul's circular rotation as the motion most appropriate to intelligence. Republic VII (528a-530c) discusses astronomy as a mathematical science of circular motions. The starting premise -- that some things are in motion, some at rest -- recalls Parmenides' distinction between being and becoming, which Plato synthesizes rather than choosing between.

narrative: "'Very true.' And when you speak of bodies moving in many places, you seem to me to mean those which move from one place to another, and sometimes have one centre of motion and sometimes more than one because they turn upon their axis; and whenever they meet anything, if it be stationary, they are divided by it; but if they get in the midst between bodies which are approaching and moving towards the same spot from opposite directions, they unite with them. 'I admit the truth of what you are saying.' Also when they unite they grow, and when they are divided they waste away -- that is, supposing the constitution of each to remain, or if that fails, then there is a second reason of their dissolution. 'And when are all things created and how?' Clearly, they are created when the first principle receives increase and attains to the second dimension, and from this arrives at the one which is neighbour to this, and after reaching the third becomes perceptible to sense. Everything which is thus changing and moving is in process of generation; only when at rest has it real existence, but when passing into another state it is destroyed utterly. Have we not mentioned all motions that there are, and comprehended them under their kinds and numbered them with the exception, my friends, of two?" said the Athenian.
refashioned:
"Bodies that move through many places," the Athenian continued, "travel from one location to another, sometimes turning on a single axis, sometimes on more than one. When they encounter a stationary object, they are divided by it; when they find themselves between bodies approaching from opposite directions, they merge with them.

When things combine, they grow; when divided, they diminish -- assuming their basic constitution holds. If it fails, that is another cause of dissolution.

How are things created? A first principle extends from one dimension to a second, then from the second to the third, and upon reaching three dimensions becomes perceptible to the senses. Everything in this process of change and motion is in a state of becoming; only when at rest does it have stable existence, and when it passes into another state it is destroyed entirely.

Now -- have we not catalogued and classified all the kinds of motion that exist, with the exception of two?"
elegant: Bodies moving through space divide when they hit stationary objects and merge when caught between converging ones. Growth comes from combination, decay from division. Creation occurs as a principle extends through one, two, then three dimensions until it becomes perceptible. All that changes is in the process of becoming; only what is at rest truly exists. All motions have been classified except two -- the most important ones.
summary: The Athenian continues cataloguing types of motion: locomotion, collision, division, combination, growth, decay, and the dimensional process of creation from point to line to surface to solid. He observes that only what is at rest has stable existence, while everything in motion is in a state of becoming. He notes that two crucial types of motion remain unclassified.
distilled: All types of physical motion have been catalogued -- combination, division, growth, decay, dimensional generation -- except for two: the most important kinds that relate directly to the soul.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The taxonomy of motion continues, covering locomotion, collision (division and combination), growth and decay, and dimensional generation (from point to line to plane to solid). The observation that "only when at rest has it real existence" echoes the Parmenidean and Platonic privileging of stable being over flux -- cf. Timaeus 27d-28a on the distinction between what always is and what is always becoming. The dimensional progression from first to second to third dimension recalls the mathematical cosmology of the Timaeus (53c-55c), where the physical elements are constructed from geometric shapes. The dramatic pause before revealing the two remaining motions creates suspense: these two -- motion that moves others but not itself, and self-motion -- are the philosophical climax of the entire argument.

narrative:
"Which are they?" asked Cleinias.

"Just the two, with which our present enquiry is concerned," replied the Athenian.

"Speak plainer," said Cleinias.

"I suppose that our enquiry has reference to the soul?" said the Athenian.

"Very true," agreed Cleinias.

"Let us assume that there is a motion able to move other things, but not to move itself -- that is one kind; and there is another kind which can move itself as well as other things, working in composition and decomposition, by increase and diminution and generation and destruction -- that is also one of the many kinds of motion," said the Athenian.

"Granted," said Cleinias.

"And we will assume that which moves other, and is changed by other, to be the ninth, and that which changes itself and others, and is co-incident with every action and every passion, and is the true principle of change and motion in all that is -- that we shall be inclined to call the tenth," said the Athenian.

"Certainly," agreed Cleinias.

"And which of these ten motions ought we to prefer as being the mightiest and most efficient?" asked the Athenian.

"I must say that the motion which is able to move itself is ten thousand times superior to all the others," replied Cleinias.

"Very good; but may I make one or two corrections in what I have been saying?" asked the Athenian.

"What are they?" asked Cleinias.

"When I spoke of the tenth sort of motion, that was not quite correct," said the Athenian.

"What was the error?" asked Cleinias.

"According to the true order, the tenth was really the first in generation and power; then follows the second, which was strangely enough termed the ninth by us," explained the Athenian.

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.

"I mean this: when one thing changes another, and that another, of such will there be any primary changing element? How can a thing which is moved by another ever be the beginning of change? Impossible. But when the self-moved changes other, and that again other, and thus thousands upon tens of thousands of bodies are set in motion, must not the beginning of all this motion be the change of the self-moving principle?" said the Athenian.

"Very true, and I quite agree," replied Cleinias.

"Or, to put the question in another way, making answer to ourselves: If, as most of these philosophers have the audacity to affirm, all things were at rest in one mass, which of the above-mentioned principles of motion would first spring up among them?" asked the Athenian.

"Clearly the self-moving; for there could be no change in them arising out of any external cause; the change must first take place in themselves," answered Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Which two are missing?" asked Cleinias.

"The very two our whole inquiry concerns," the Athenian replied.

"Explain more clearly."

"Our inquiry concerns the soul, does it not?"

"It does."

"Then consider: there is a kind of motion that can move other things but cannot move itself -- that is one kind. And there is another that can move both itself and other things, operating through combination and separation, increase and decrease, generation and destruction."

"Granted."

"Let us call the motion that moves others but is moved by others the ninth kind. And the motion that moves itself and others -- that coincides with every action and every experience, and is the true principle of all change and motion in everything that exists -- we shall call the tenth."

"Agreed."

"Now, which of these ten motions should we regard as the mightiest and most effective?"

"Self-motion is ten thousand times superior to all the others," Cleinias declared.

"Excellent. But let me correct something I said. When I called self-motion the tenth, that was not quite right."

"What was the error?"

"In the true order, the 'tenth' is actually the first -- first in generation and power. What we called the ninth is really the second."

"What do you mean?"

"Consider: when one thing moves another, and that moves another, can there be any starting point in such a chain? How can something moved by another ever be the origin of change? Impossible. But when the self-moved sets another in motion, and that sets another, and so on through thousands upon tens of thousands -- the origin of all that motion must be the self-moving principle."

"That is certainly true."

"Or put it another way: if, as these materialist philosophers audaciously claim, all things were once at rest in a single undifferentiated mass -- which of our ten kinds of motion would first arise?"

"Obviously self-motion," Cleinias answered. "No change could arise from any external cause in such a state. The first change must originate from within."
elegant: The two crucial types of motion: that which moves others but not itself (ninth), and that which moves both itself and others (tenth). Self-motion is infinitely superior -- and though numbered last, it is actually first in the order of existence. In any chain of caused motions, the origin must be a self-mover. If everything were once at rest, only self-motion could initiate change. Therefore self-motion is the first principle of all motion.
summary: The Athenian reveals the two remaining types of motion: motion that moves other things but not itself, and self-motion that moves both itself and others. Self-motion, though numbered tenth, is actually first in the true order -- since no chain of externally caused motions can have a beginning unless a self-mover initiates it. Even from a state of universal rest, only self-motion could arise first.
distilled: Self-motion is the first principle of all motion: no chain of externally caused changes can begin without a self-mover, and from universal rest only self-motion could arise.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. This is the philosophical heart of Book 10 -- the proof that self-motion is the first principle. The argument proceeds in two steps: (1) regress argument -- any chain of moved movers requires an unmoved (self-moving) origin; (2) cosmogonic argument -- from a hypothetical state of universal rest, only self-motion could initiate change. The numbering reversal (tenth becomes first) is a deliberate dramatic device emphasizing that what seemed last in enumeration is first in reality -- mirroring the materialists' inversion that the argument corrects. Cleinias's exclamation that self-motion is "ten thousand times superior" uses the Greek idiom murioplasion, expressing vast superiority. Cross-reference: Phaedrus 245c-246a presents the same argument: "All soul is immortal, for that which is ever in motion is immortal. But that which while imparting motion is itself moved by something else can cease to move and therefore cease to live." Aristotle's Unmoved Mover (Metaphysics XII, Physics VIII) develops from and transforms this Platonic argument, replacing self-motion with a motionless final cause.

narrative:
"Then we must say that self-motion being the origin of all motions, and the first which arises among things at rest as well as among things in motion, is the eldest and mightiest principle of change, and that which is changed by another and yet moves other is second," said the Athenian.

"Quite true," agreed Cleinias.

"At this stage of the argument let us put a question," said the Athenian.

"What question?" asked Cleinias.

"If we were to see this power existing in any earthy, watery, or fiery substance, simple or compound -- how should we describe it?" asked the Athenian.

"You mean to ask whether we should call such a self-moving power life?" said Cleinias.

"I do," confirmed the Athenian.

"Certainly we should," said Cleinias.

"And when we see soul in anything, must we not do the same -- must we not admit that this is life?" asked the Athenian.

"We must," agreed Cleinias.

"And now, I beseech you, reflect; you would admit that we have a threefold knowledge of things?" said the Athenian.

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.

"I mean that we know the essence, and that we know the definition of the essence, and the name -- these are the three; and there are two questions which may be raised about anything," said the Athenian.

"How two?" asked Cleinias.

"Sometimes a person may give the name and ask the definition; or he may give the definition and ask the name. I may illustrate what I mean in this way," said the Athenian.

"How?" asked Cleinias.

"Number like some other things is capable of being divided into equal parts; when thus divided, number is named 'even,' and the definition of the name 'even' is 'number divisible into two equal parts,'" said the Athenian.

"True," agreed Cleinias.

"I mean, that when we are asked about the definition and give the name, or when we are asked about the name and give the definition -- in either case, whether we give name or definition, we speak of the same thing, calling 'even' the number which is divided into two equal parts," said the Athenian.

"Quite true," said Cleinias.

"And what is the definition of that which is named 'soul'? Can we conceive of any other than that which has been already given -- the motion which can move itself?" asked the Athenian.

"You mean to say that the essence which is defined as the self-moved is the same with that which has the name soul?" said Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Self-motion, then, is the origin of all motions," the Athenian concluded. "It is the first to arise whether among things at rest or things already in motion -- the eldest and mightiest principle of change. Motion that is caused by another while itself causing motion in something else is second."

"Exactly right," said Cleinias.

"Now let us ask a question. If we observed this power of self-motion in any earthy, watery, or fiery substance, whether simple or compound -- what would we call it?"

"You are asking whether we would call that power 'life,'" Cleinias anticipated.

"I am."

"Of course we would."

"And when we recognize soul in anything, must we not acknowledge the same thing -- that where there is soul, there is life?"

"We must."

"Now reflect on this: we have three ways of knowing any given thing -- its essence, its definition, and its name. And two questions can be asked about anything: given the name, what is the definition? Or given the definition, what is the name?"

"Go on."

"Take number as an example. A number divisible into two equal parts is named 'even.' The definition of 'even' is 'a number divisible into two equal parts.' Whether you start from the name or from the definition, you arrive at the same thing."

"True."

"Now -- what is the definition of that which bears the name 'soul'? Can we conceive any definition other than what we have already established: the motion that can move itself?"

"You are saying," Cleinias replied, "that the essence defined as 'self-moved' is identical with what we call 'soul.'"
elegant: Self-motion is the first and mightiest principle of all change. Wherever we see self-moving power in any substance, we call it life. Where there is soul, there is life. Every thing has an essence, a definition, and a name. Just as 'even' is defined as 'number divisible into two equal parts,' so 'soul' is defined as 'self-moving motion.' The essence of soul is self-motion.
summary: The Athenian establishes that self-motion is the origin of all motion, then connects it to life and soul through a careful semantic analysis. Self-moving power in any substance is called life; soul is wherever life is; and the very definition of 'soul' is 'the motion that moves itself.' The essence, definition, and name all converge on the same identity.
distilled: Soul is defined as self-motion -- wherever we find the power to move itself, there we find life, and there we find soul.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. The argument here moves from physics to ontology to semantics: (1) self-motion is the first principle of all motion, (2) self-motion in any substance is called life, (3) the name for this life-principle is 'soul,' (4) therefore soul = self-motion by definition. The semantic analysis using essence/definition/name is an application of the method developed in the Sophist (218b-221c) and Cratylus (383a-390e). The example of 'even' as 'number divisible into two equal parts' demonstrates that name and definition pick out the same essence -- applied to soul, this yields the crucial identity: soul = self-moving motion. Cross-reference: Phaedrus 245e: "Soul, whose definition is self-motion." This definition of soul as self-motion is Plato's most enduring contribution to psychology (literally, the study of psyche). Aristotle will modify it in De Anima II.1, defining soul instead as the "first actuality of a natural body having life potentially."

narrative:
"Yes; and if this is true, do we still maintain that there is anything wanting in the proof that the soul is the first origin and moving power of all that is, or has become, or will be, and their contraries, when she has been clearly shown to be the source of change and motion in all things?" asked the Athenian.

"Certainly not; the soul as being the source of motion, has been most satisfactorily shown to be the oldest of all things," agreed Cleinias.

"And is not that motion which is produced in another, by reason of another, but never has any self-moving power at all, being in truth the change of an inanimate body, to be reckoned second, or by any lower number which you may prefer?" asked the Athenian.

"Exactly," said Cleinias.

"Then we are right, and speak the most perfect and absolute truth, when we say that the soul is prior to the body, and that the body is second and comes afterwards, and is born to obey the soul, which is the ruler?" said the Athenian.

"Nothing can be more true," affirmed Cleinias.

"Do you remember our old admission, that if the soul was prior to the body the things of the soul were also prior to those of the body?" asked the Athenian.

"Certainly," said Cleinias.

"Then characters and manners, and wishes and reasonings, and true opinions, and reflections, and recollections are prior to length and breadth and depth and strength of bodies, if the soul is prior to the body," said the Athenian.

"To be sure," agreed Cleinias.

"In the next place, must we not of necessity admit that the soul is the cause of good and evil, base and honourable, just and unjust, and of all other opposites, if we suppose her to be the cause of all things?" asked the Athenian.

"We must," said Cleinias.

"And as the soul orders and inhabits all things that move, however moving, must we not say that she orders also the heavens?" asked the Athenian.

"Of course," agreed Cleinias.

"One soul or more? More than one -- I will answer for you; at any rate, we must not suppose that there are less than two -- one the author of good, and the other of evil," said the Athenian.

"Very true," agreed Cleinias.
refashioned:
"If soul is self-motion, and self-motion is the source of all other motion," the Athenian asked, "is anything lacking in the proof that soul is the first origin and moving power of all that is, has been, or will be -- and of their opposites as well?"

"Nothing is lacking," Cleinias confirmed. "The soul has been shown most convincingly to be the oldest of all things."

"And motion produced in one thing by another -- motion with no self-moving power whatsoever, the mere change of inanimate body -- that must be ranked second, or lower still?"

"Exactly."

"Then we speak the most perfect and absolute truth when we say this: the soul is prior to the body. The body is second, comes afterward, and is born to obey the soul, which is its ruler."

"Nothing could be more true."

"And you recall our earlier agreement -- that if soul is prior to body, then the attributes of soul must also be prior to the attributes of body?"

"I do."

"Then character, disposition, wishes, reasoning, true opinions, reflections, and memories all precede length, breadth, depth, and physical strength."

"Certainly."

"Furthermore, if soul is the cause of all things, must we not admit that soul is the cause of good and evil, noble and base, just and unjust, and every other pair of opposites?"

"We must."

"And since soul orders and inhabits all things that move -- in whatever way they move -- must we not say that soul also orders the heavens?"

"Of course."

"One soul, or more? I will answer for you: more than one. We must suppose at least two -- one the author of good, the other of evil."

"That is right," Cleinias agreed.
elegant: The proof is complete: soul, as self-motion, is the first origin and power behind all that exists. Body is secondary and born to obey soul. Therefore all attributes of soul -- character, reasoning, opinion, memory -- precede all physical properties. Soul is the cause of good and evil, justice and injustice. Soul orders the heavens. There must be at least two souls: one the author of good, the other of evil.
summary: The Athenian draws the full consequences of soul's priority: soul is the oldest thing, body is secondary and subordinate, psychological attributes precede physical ones, and soul is the cause of all moral opposites. Since soul governs everything that moves -- including the heavens -- there must be at least two cosmic souls: one responsible for good, another for evil.
distilled: Soul is the first origin of all things, prior to body in every respect; it governs the heavens, and there must be at least two cosmic souls -- one authoring good, the other evil.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This passage draws out the full metaphysical, moral, and cosmological implications of the proof of soul's priority. The claim that "the body is born to obey the soul, which is the ruler" establishes the political metaphor central to Plato's psychology (Republic IV, 441e-442a). The priority of psychological attributes over physical ones (character over length, reasoning over breadth) reverses the materialist hierarchy definitively. The introduction of at least two cosmic souls -- one good, one evil -- is remarkable and somewhat unique in Plato. It recalls the Zoroastrian dualism of Ahura Mazda and Angra Mainyu, and anticipates later Gnostic and Manichaean cosmologies. In the Timaeus, there is one good Demiurge; here the Athenian acknowledges an evil cosmic principle as well. Cross-reference: Republic II (379b-380c) insists that god is the cause only of good; the evil soul here may represent the Necessity (ananke) that the Demiurge must persuade in Timaeus 48a. Phaedrus 246a-b similarly speaks of souls that follow the gods and others that fall.

narrative: "Yes, very true; the soul then directs all things in heaven, and earth, and sea by her movements, and these are described by the terms -- will, consideration, attention, deliberation, opinion true and false, joy and sorrow, confidence, fear, hatred, love, and other primary motions akin to these; which again receive the secondary motions of corporeal substances, and guide all things to growth and decay, to composition and decomposition, and to the qualities which accompany them, such as heat and cold, heaviness and lightness, hardness and softness, blackness and whiteness, bitterness and sweetness, and all those other qualities which the soul uses, herself a goddess, when truly receiving the divine mind she disciplines all things rightly to their happiness; but when she is the companion of folly, she does the very contrary of all this. Shall we assume so much, or do we still entertain doubts?" said the Athenian.
refashioned:
"The soul, then, directs all things in heaven, earth, and sea through her movements," the Athenian continued. "These movements bear names we know: will, consideration, attention, deliberation, true and false opinion, joy and sorrow, confidence, fear, hatred, love, and other primary motions of the same kind.

These psychic movements in turn take up the secondary, physical motions of material substances and guide all things toward growth and decay, composition and decomposition, and the qualities that accompany these processes -- heat and cold, heaviness and lightness, hardness and softness, dark and light, bitter and sweet, and every other sensory quality.

The soul uses all of these. When she truly receives divine mind, she is herself a goddess, ordering all things rightly toward their happiness. But when she is the companion of folly, she produces the very opposite of all this.

Shall we accept this, or do we still have doubts?"
elegant: Soul directs everything in heaven, earth, and sea through primary psychic motions -- will, deliberation, opinion, joy, fear, love, hatred -- which in turn govern the secondary physical motions of matter: growth and decay, heat and cold, hard and soft. When soul receives divine mind, she orders all things rightly toward happiness, like a goddess. When she follows folly, she produces the opposite.
summary: The Athenian describes soul's complete governance: psychic motions (will, deliberation, opinion, emotion) are primary and direct the secondary physical motions of matter (growth, decay, and all sensory qualities). When soul is guided by divine mind, it orders everything toward happiness; when it follows folly, it produces the opposite.
distilled: Soul governs the cosmos through primary psychic motions -- will, thought, emotion -- which direct all secondary physical processes; guided by divine mind, soul brings happiness, but guided by folly, it brings ruin.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This passage provides the most comprehensive account of soul's causal sovereignty in all of Plato. The hierarchy is precise: primary motions are psychic (will, deliberation, opinion, emotion), and secondary motions are physical (growth, decay, sensory qualities). This directly inverts the materialist claim that physical processes are primary and psychic phenomena derivative. The description of soul as "herself a goddess" (aute theos ousa) when united with divine mind is extraordinary -- it divinizes soul itself, not just the gods. The contrast between soul receiving "divine mind" (theios nous) and soul as "companion of folly" (anoia) establishes two modes of cosmic governance corresponding to the two souls of 10.24. Cross-reference: Timaeus 30b describes the Demiurge placing nous in soul and soul in body. Philebus 30c-d argues that cosmic wisdom governs the universe. The list of psychic motions (will, deliberation, opinion, joy, sorrow, etc.) is the most detailed taxonomy of mental acts in Plato, anticipating Aristotle's De Anima and later faculty psychology.

narrative:
"There is no room at all for doubt," said Cleinias.

"Shall we say then that it is the soul which controls heaven and earth, and the whole world? -- that it is a principle of wisdom and virtue, or a principle which has neither wisdom nor virtue? Suppose that we make answer as follows," said the Athenian.

"How would you answer?" asked Cleinias.

"If, my friend, we say that the whole path and movement of heaven, and of all that is therein, is by nature akin to the movement and revolution and calculation of mind, and proceeds by kindred laws, then, as is plain, we must say that the best soul takes care of the world and guides it along the good path," said the Athenian.

"True," agreed Cleinias.

"But if the world moves wildly and irregularly, then the evil soul guides it," continued the Athenian.

"True again," said Cleinias.

"Of what nature is the movement of mind? To this question it is not easy to give an intelligent answer; and therefore I ought to assist you in framing one," said the Athenian.

"Very good," said Cleinias.

"Then let us not answer as if we would look straight at the sun, making ourselves darkness at midday -- I mean as if we were under the impression that we could see with mortal eyes, or know adequately the nature of mind; it will be safer to look at the image only," said the Athenian.

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.

"Let us select of the ten motions the one which mind chiefly resembles; this I will bring to your recollection, and will then make the answer on behalf of us all," said the Athenian.

"That will be excellent," said Cleinias.

"You will surely remember our saying that all things were either at rest or in motion?" asked the Athenian.

"I do," confirmed Cleinias.

"And that of things in motion some were moving in one place, and others in more than one?" continued the Athenian.

"Yes," said Cleinias.

"Of these two kinds of motion, that which moves in one place must move about a centre like globes made in a lathe, and is most entirely akin and similar to the circular movement of mind," said the Athenian.

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.

"In saying that both mind and the motion which is in one place move in the same and like manner, in and about the same, and in relation to the same, and according to one proportion and order, and are like the motion of a globe, we invented a fair image, which does no discredit to our ingenuity," said the Athenian.

"It does us great credit," agreed Cleinias.
refashioned:
"There is no room for doubt," Cleinias affirmed.

"Then the question becomes: Is the soul that controls heaven, earth, and the entire world a principle of wisdom and virtue, or one devoid of both? Let me propose an answer."

"Please do."

"If the entire path and movement of heaven and everything in it is by nature akin to the movement, revolution, and calculation of mind -- if it proceeds according to the same laws that govern rational thought -- then we must conclude that the best soul governs the world and guides it along the good path."

"That follows."

"But if the world moves wildly and without order, then an evil soul governs it."

"That follows too."

"Now, what is the nature of mind's movement? This is not easy to answer well, so let me help frame a response. We must not try to look directly at mind any more than we would stare straight at the sun -- we would only blind ourselves at midday. Mortal eyes cannot perceive the nature of mind directly. It is safer to examine an image of it."

"What image?"

"Let us find, among our ten types of motion, the one that most resembles mind. Recall that all things are either at rest or in motion, and that things in motion either move in one place or through many places. Motion in one place -- rotation about a centre, like a globe on a lathe -- is the motion most perfectly akin to the circular movement of mind."

"How so?"

"Both mind and rotational motion move in the same manner, about the same centre, in relation to the same point, according to consistent proportion and order. The spinning globe is a fair image of rational thought -- and no discredit to our reasoning."

"It does us great credit," Cleinias agreed.
elegant: Does a wise soul or a foolish one govern the cosmos? The answer depends on the nature of heavenly motion. If the heavens move with the regularity, proportion, and order characteristic of rational thought, then the best soul guides them. If chaotically, an evil soul does. Mind's motion most resembles rotation about a fixed centre -- uniform, proportional, orderly, like a globe on a lathe. This circular motion is the image of rational thought, and the heavens display it perfectly.
summary: The Athenian asks whether the cosmic soul is wise or foolish, and proposes a test: if the heavens move with rational order, a good soul governs; if chaotically, an evil one. He identifies circular rotation as the best image of mind's movement -- uniform, centred, proportional -- and shows that the heavens exhibit exactly this kind of motion.
distilled: The orderly circular motion of the heavens mirrors the circular movement of rational mind, proving that the best soul -- not a chaotic one -- governs the cosmos.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. The analogy between mind and circular motion is one of Plato's most celebrated philosophical images. The warning against looking directly at mind (as at the sun) recalls Republic VI (508b-509a), where the Form of the Good is compared to the sun and cannot be apprehended directly. The identification of mind with uniform circular motion draws on Pythagorean astronomy and is developed extensively in Timaeus 34a and 36c-37a, where the World Soul rotates in perfect circles. The lathe-turned globe as image of mind anticipates the medieval concept of the celestial spheres as embodiments of intelligence. The argument is empirical in structure: we observe the heavens moving with regular, proportional order; this order resembles the motion of mind; therefore mind (good soul) governs the heavens. Cross-reference: Aristotle's celestial mechanics in De Caelo develop this Platonic insight, and his Unmoved Mover (Metaphysics XII) is characterized by eternal self-contemplation -- circular intellectual activity.

narrative:
"And the motion of the other sort which is not after the same manner, nor in the same, nor about the same, nor in relation to the same, nor in one place, nor in order, nor according to any rule or proportion, may be said to be akin to senselessness and folly?" asked the Athenian.

"That is most true," agreed Cleinias.

"Then, after what has been said, there is no difficulty in distinctly stating, that since soul carries all things round, either the best soul or the contrary must of necessity carry round and order and arrange the revolution of the heaven," said the Athenian.

"And judging from what has been said, Stranger, there would be impiety in asserting that any but the most perfect soul or souls carries round the heavens," said Cleinias.

"You have understood my meaning right well, Cleinias, and now let me ask you another question," said the Athenian.

"What are you going to ask?" asked Cleinias.

"If the soul carries round the sun and moon, and the other stars, does she not carry round each individual of them?" asked the Athenian.

"Certainly," said Cleinias.

"Then of one of them let us speak, and the same argument will apply to all," said the Athenian.

"Which will you take?" asked Cleinias.

"Every one sees the body of the sun, but no one sees his soul, nor the soul of any other body living or dead; and yet there is great reason to believe that this nature, unperceived by any of our senses, is circumfused around them all, but is perceived by mind; and therefore by mind and reflection only let us apprehend the following point," said the Athenian.

"What is that?" asked Cleinias.

"If the soul carries round the sun, we shall not be far wrong in supposing one of three alternatives," said the Athenian.

"What are they?" asked Cleinias.

"Either the soul which moves the sun this way and that, resides within the circular and visible body, like the soul which carries us about every way; or the soul provides herself with an external body of fire or air, as some affirm, and violently propels body by body; or thirdly, she is without such a body, but guides the sun by some extraordinary and wonderful power," said the Athenian.

"Yes, certainly; the soul can only order all things in one of these three ways," agreed Cleinias.
refashioned:
"And motion of the opposite sort -- erratic, disordered, without fixed centre, proportion, or rule -- would that not be akin to senselessness and folly?" the Athenian asked.

"Absolutely," said Cleinias.

"Then the conclusion is clear: since soul moves all things in their courses, either the best soul or its opposite must be responsible for the revolution of the heavens."

"Given everything we have said, Stranger, it would be impious to claim that anything less than the most perfect soul governs the heavens."

"You understand me perfectly, Cleinias. Now consider this: if soul moves the heavens as a whole, does she not also move each celestial body individually -- the sun, the moon, every star?"

"She must."

"Then let us take one example, and the same reasoning will apply to all. Take the sun. Everyone sees its body, but no one sees its soul -- nor the soul of any living or dead thing. Yet there is strong reason to believe that this invisible nature -- perceptible only to mind, not to the senses -- is diffused around all celestial bodies. So let us grasp the following point through reason alone."

"What point?"

"If soul moves the sun, one of three things must be true. Either the soul resides within the sun's visible body, as our souls reside within us and carry us about. Or the soul provides itself with an outer body of fire or air and propels the sun from outside, body pushing body. Or third, the soul has no body at all but guides the sun through some extraordinary and wonderful power."

"The soul can govern the sun only in one of these three ways," Cleinias agreed.
elegant: Erratic, disordered motion signifies folly; ordered circular motion signifies mind. Since the heavens move with perfect order, only the best soul can govern them. This applies to each celestial body individually. The sun's soul -- invisible to sense but perceptible to mind -- either dwells within the sun's body (as our souls dwell in us), propels it from outside through an intermediate body, or guides it through a bodiless, extraordinary power.
summary: The Athenian contrasts ordered motion (mind) with chaotic motion (folly) and concludes that only the most perfect soul can govern the orderly heavens. He then applies this to individual celestial bodies, proposing three ways the sun's soul might move it: from within like our souls move our bodies, from outside through an intermediate element, or through a purely immaterial power.
distilled: The perfectly ordered motion of each celestial body proves it is governed by a perfect soul, which either inhabits the body, propels it from outside, or guides it through immaterial power.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. The three modes by which soul might govern the sun anticipate centuries of debate about the relationship between soul/mind and body. Mode one (soul within body) is the model of embodied soul familiar from ordinary experience. Mode two (soul with an external body pushing the sun) reflects some pre-Socratic physical theories. Mode three (soul without body guiding through immaterial power) is the most metaphysically radical and anticipates later Neoplatonic and Christian theology of incorporeal divine action. The Athenian deliberately leaves the choice open -- what matters is that soul, not matter, is the governing cause. The claim that soul is "circumfused" around celestial bodies but perceived only by mind connects to the epistemology of the Divided Line (Republic VI, 509d-511e), where the highest objects of knowledge are apprehended by nous alone. Cross-reference: Timaeus 36d-e describes the World Soul enveloping the World Body. Epinomis 981b-985b (attributed to Plato or Philip of Opus) develops the astral theology of this passage in detail.

narrative:
"And this soul of the sun, which is therefore better than the sun, whether taking the sun about in a chariot to give light to men, or acting from without or in whatever way, ought by every man to be deemed a God," said the Athenian.

"Yes, by every man who has the least particle of sense," agreed Cleinias.
refashioned:
"This soul of the sun -- which is superior to the sun itself -- whether it carries the sun in a chariot to give light to mankind, or acts upon it from without, or governs it in any other manner, must be regarded by every person as a god," the Athenian declared.

"By every person with the least particle of sense," Cleinias agreed.
elegant: The soul that moves the sun is greater than the sun itself. However it governs the sun -- whether from within, from without, or by immaterial power -- that soul must be acknowledged as a god by every rational person.
summary: The Athenian declares that the soul governing the sun is superior to the sun and must be recognized as a god, regardless of the specific mechanism by which it exercises control. Cleinias emphatically agrees.
distilled: The soul that moves the sun is greater than the sun and must be acknowledged as a god by every rational person.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This is the culmination of the proof of the gods' existence: from the priority of soul over body, to the identification of cosmic souls governing celestial bodies, to the explicit theological conclusion that these souls are gods. The chariot image evokes Helios driving his chariot across the sky -- traditional mythology reinterpreted through philosophical theology. The argument is significant because it establishes divinity not through myth or tradition but through rational demonstration: the sun's soul is a god because it is an intelligent, self-moving cause superior to the physical body it governs. Cross-reference: Phaedrus 246e-247a uses the chariot metaphor for the soul's ascent to divine contemplation. Republic VI (508a-509b) makes the sun an image of the Form of the Good. The Epinomis (983b-984b) develops this astral theology further, identifying the visible gods as celestial bodies animated by divine souls.

narrative: "And of the stars too, and of the moon, and of the years and months and seasons, must we not say in like manner, that since a soul or souls having every sort of excellence are the causes of all of them, those souls are Gods, whether they are living beings and reside in bodies, and in this way order the whole heaven, or whatever be the place and mode of their existence; and will any one who admits all this venture to deny that all things are full of Gods?" said the Athenian.
refashioned: "And must we not say the same of the stars, the moon, and the years and months and seasons?" the Athenian continued. "Since souls possessing every kind of excellence are the causes of all these things, those souls are gods -- whether they are living beings residing within bodies and thus ordering the whole heaven, or whether they exist in some other place and manner. And will anyone who accepts all of this still dare to deny that all things are full of gods?"
elegant: What is true of the sun is true of every star, the moon, and the seasons: each is governed by a soul of perfect excellence, and each such soul is a god. Whether these divine souls inhabit celestial bodies or exist in some other mode, the conclusion is inescapable: all things are full of gods.
summary: The Athenian extends the argument from the sun to all celestial bodies and the cycles of time: every star, the moon, the seasons, and temporal cycles are governed by excellent souls, and those souls are gods. The conclusion echoes Thales: all things are full of gods.
distilled: Every celestial body and temporal cycle is governed by an excellent soul that is a god -- therefore all things are full of gods.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The concluding phrase "all things are full of gods" (panta plere theon) is traditionally attributed to Thales of Miletus (Aristotle, De Anima 411a7), the earliest Greek philosopher. Plato here reclaims this saying from its pre-Socratic context and gives it a new, philosophically grounded meaning: the world is full of gods not because of vague pantheistic intuition but because demonstrably rational souls govern every cosmic process. The extension from sun to stars, moon, and temporal cycles makes the theology comprehensive. The qualification "whether they reside in bodies or exist in some other mode" preserves philosophical flexibility about the precise nature of divine embodiment. Cross-reference: Timaeus 40a-b describes the fixed stars as eternal living beings, "divine and everlasting animals." Epinomis 984d-985b develops a hierarchy of celestial divinities. The phrase also anticipates Stoic pantheism, where divine logos pervades all matter.

narrative:
"No one, Stranger, would be such a madman," said Cleinias.

"And now, Megillus and Cleinias, let us offer terms to him who has hitherto denied the existence of the Gods, and leave him," said the Athenian.

"What terms?" asked Cleinias.

"Either he shall teach us that we were wrong in saying that the soul is the original of all things, and arguing accordingly; or, if he be not able to say anything better, then he must yield to us and live for the remainder of his life in the belief that there are Gods. Let us see, then, whether we have said enough or not enough to those who deny that there are Gods," said the Athenian.

"Certainly -- quite enough, Stranger," replied Cleinias.
refashioned:
"No sane person would deny it, Stranger," said Cleinias.

"Then let us offer terms to those who have denied the gods' existence, and move on," the Athenian proposed.

"What terms?"

"Either they must demonstrate that we were wrong to claim that soul is the origin of all things and to argue as we have -- or, if they cannot offer a better account, they must yield and live the rest of their lives believing that gods exist. So let us ask: have we said enough to those who deny the gods?"

"Quite enough, Stranger," Cleinias confirmed.
elegant: The terms to the atheist: either refute the proof that soul is the origin of all things, or accept the existence of gods and live accordingly. The burden of proof has shifted. The first of the three impieties -- outright atheism -- has been answered.
summary: The Athenian concludes the proof against atheism by offering the denier a choice: either refute the argument that soul is the origin of all things, or accept the existence of the gods. Cleinias confirms the argument is sufficient, and the discussion moves on to the second form of impiety.
distilled: The atheist must either refute the proof that soul is the origin of all things or yield and live believing in the gods -- the case against outright denial is complete.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. This marks the formal conclusion of the first and longest argument of Book 10: the proof of the gods' existence through the priority of soul. The rhetorical move -- offering the atheist terms like a defeated party in negotiation -- gives the conclusion legal as well as philosophical force, appropriate for a work on legislation. The burden of proof is explicitly shifted to the atheist: the default position is now theism, and atheism requires a positive counter-argument. Cross-reference: the structure mirrors the Republic's treatment of Thrasymachus, who is offered the choice of maintaining his position or conceding (Republic I, 350d-354c). The three-part structure of Book 10 (atheism, divine negligence, divine corruptibility) now transitions from the first to the second. Aristotle similarly concludes physical arguments with challenges to the opponent in Topics and Physics.

narrative: "Then to them we will say no more. And now we are to address him who, believing that there are Gods, believes also that they take no heed of human affairs: To him we say -- 'O thou best of men, in believing that there are Gods you are led by some affinity to them, which attracts you towards your kindred and makes you honour and believe in them. But the fortunes of evil and unrighteous men in private as well as public life, which, though not really happy, are wrongly counted happy in the judgment of men, and are celebrated both by poets and prose writers -- these draw you aside from your natural piety. Perhaps you have seen impious men growing old and leaving their children's children in high offices, and their prosperity shakes your faith -- you have known or heard or been yourself an eyewitness of many monstrous impieties, and have beheld men by such criminal means from small beginnings attaining to sovereignty and the pinnacle of greatness; and considering all these things you do not like to accuse the Gods of them, because they are your relatives; and so from some want of reasoning power, and also from an unwillingness to find fault with them, you have come to believe that they exist indeed, but have no thought or care of human things. Now, that your present evil opinion may not grow to still greater impiety, and that we may if possible use arguments which may conjure away the evil before it arrives, we will add another argument to that originally addressed to him who utterly denied the existence of the Gods. And do you, Megillus and Cleinias, answer for the young man as you did before; and if any impediment comes in our way, I will take the word out of your mouths, and carry you over the river as I did just now,'" said the Athenian.
refashioned:
"We will say no more to the atheist. Now we must address the person who believes gods exist but thinks they take no interest in human affairs.

To this person we say: 'You believe in the gods because a natural affinity draws you toward them -- your kinship with the divine inclines you to honour and trust them. But the apparent prosperity of wicked people has shaken your faith. You have seen unprincipled men in both private and public life flourish, accumulating wealth and power despite their crimes. You have watched the impious grow old in comfort and leave their grandchildren in positions of authority. You have witnessed -- or heard reliable accounts of -- people rising from nothing to sovereignty through monstrous acts.

Because you feel a kinship with the gods, you cannot bring yourself to blame them directly. So instead, through a weakness of reasoning combined with reluctance to criticize the divine, you have arrived at the conclusion that the gods exist but simply do not concern themselves with human affairs.

To prevent this error from growing into something worse, we will add a new argument to the one already made against outright atheism.'

Megillus and Cleinias, please continue answering on behalf of our hypothetical young man. If the argument becomes too difficult, I will step in and carry you across the river as before."
elegant: The Athenian now addresses the second impiety: the belief that gods exist but are indifferent to humanity. This view, he explains, arises not from malice but from the troubling spectacle of wicked people prospering. When the impious flourish and the pious suffer, faith in divine justice wavers. Combined with reluctance to blame the gods one feels kinship with, this leads to the conclusion that gods simply do not care about human affairs.
summary: The Athenian turns to the second form of impiety: belief in gods who are indifferent to human affairs. He sympathetically diagnoses its cause -- the apparent prosperity of the wicked shakes faith in divine justice, and reluctance to blame the gods leads to the conclusion that they simply do not care. He promises a new argument to address this view.
distilled: Belief in divine indifference arises when the apparent prosperity of the wicked shakes faith in divine justice, and reluctance to blame the gods leads to the conclusion that they simply do not care about human affairs.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. This begins the second major argument of Book 10, addressing divine negligence. The diagnosis of the cause is psychologically subtle: the deist (who believes gods exist but do not care) is more sympathetic than the atheist, motivated not by intellectual arrogance but by the problem of evil -- the oldest and most persistent challenge to theism. The observation that wicked people prosper while the pious suffer is the central problem of theodicy, explored in depth in the Hebrew Book of Job and later Christian theology. The Athenian identifies two causes: (1) the empirical observation of unjust prosperity, and (2) an emotional reluctance to blame gods one feels akin to. Cross-reference: Republic II (363a-367e) presents Adeimantus's challenge that unjust people prosper while the just suffer. Gorgias (472d-479e) argues that the wicked are not truly happy despite appearances. The river-crossing metaphor from 10.19 recurs, maintaining the thread of pedagogical care.

narrative:
"Very good; do as you say, and we will help you as well as we can," said Cleinias.

"There will probably be no difficulty in proving to him that the Gods care about the small as well as about the great. For he was present and heard what was said, that they are perfectly good, and that the care of all things is most entirely natural to them," said the Athenian.

"No doubt he heard that," agreed Cleinias.

"Let us consider together in the next place what we mean by this virtue which we ascribe to them. Surely we should say that to be temperate and to possess mind belongs to virtue, and the contrary to vice?" asked the Athenian.

"Certainly," said Cleinias.

"Yes; and courage is a part of virtue, and cowardice of vice?" continued the Athenian.

"True," agreed Cleinias.

"And the one is honourable, and the other dishonourable?" asked the Athenian.

"To be sure," said Cleinias.

"And the one, like other meaner things, is a human quality, but the Gods have no part in anything of the sort?" said the Athenian.

"That again is what everybody will admit," agreed Cleinias.

"But do we imagine carelessness and idleness and luxury to be virtues? What do you think?" asked the Athenian.

"Decidedly not," said Cleinias.

"They rank under the opposite class?" asked the Athenian.

"Yes," said Cleinias.

"And their opposites, therefore, would fall under the opposite class?" continued the Athenian.

"Yes," agreed Cleinias.

"But are we to suppose that one who possesses all these good qualities will be luxurious and heedless and idle, like those whom the poet compares to stingless drones?" asked the Athenian.

"And the comparison is a most just one," said Cleinias.

"Surely God must not be supposed to have a nature which he himself hates? He who dares to say this sort of thing must not be tolerated for a moment," said the Athenian.

"Of course not. How could he have?" agreed Cleinias.

"Should we not on any principle be entirely mistaken in praising any one who has some special business entrusted to him, if he have a mind which takes care of great matters and no care of small ones? Reflect; he who acts in this way, whether he be God or man, must act from one of two principles," said the Athenian.

"What are they?" asked Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Very well -- do as you say, and we will help however we can," said Cleinias.

"It should not be difficult to show that the gods care about small matters as well as great," the Athenian began. "Our interlocutor has already heard that the gods are perfectly good and that caring for all things comes naturally to them."

"Indeed he has."

"Then let us examine what we mean by the virtue we attribute to the gods. Would we not say that temperance and the possession of mind belong to virtue, and their opposites to vice?"

"Certainly."

"And courage is part of virtue, cowardice part of vice?"

"True."

"The one is honourable, the other disgraceful?"

"Of course."

"Cowardice and its kin are human weaknesses -- the gods have no share in anything of the sort?"

"Everyone would agree."

"And do we consider carelessness, idleness, and self-indulgence to be virtues?"

"Absolutely not."

"They belong to vice?"

"Yes."

"And their opposites -- attentiveness, diligence, discipline -- belong to virtue?"

"Yes."

"Then can we imagine a being who possesses every good quality being idle, heedless, and self-indulgent -- like those stingless drones the poet describes?"

"The comparison is perfectly apt."

"Surely we cannot attribute to god a nature that god himself would despise. Anyone who dares suggest such a thing should not be tolerated."

"Of course not -- how could god have such a nature?"

"And would we not be completely wrong to praise anyone entrusted with an important responsibility if they attended to great matters but neglected small ones? Anyone who behaves this way -- whether god or human -- must be acting from one of two motives."

"What are they?"
elegant: The gods are perfectly good. Virtue includes temperance, mind, and courage; vice includes carelessness, idleness, and cowardice. Gods share none of these vices. A god cannot have a nature that god himself would despise. Therefore divine neglect of small matters is incompatible with divine virtue. Anyone -- god or human -- who attends to great things while ignoring small ones must be acting from one of two motives.
summary: The Athenian argues against divine negligence through an analysis of virtue. Since the gods are perfectly good, they cannot possess the vices of carelessness, idleness, or cowardice. A god cannot have a nature it would despise. Therefore, neglecting small matters while attending to great ones is incompatible with divine perfection. Such selective neglect must stem from one of two possible causes.
distilled: Since the gods are perfectly virtuous and cannot possess the vices of carelessness or indolence, divine neglect of small matters is logically impossible -- it would contradict their own nature.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. The argument proceeds from divine perfection to the impossibility of divine neglect: if gods are perfectly virtuous, and carelessness is a vice, then gods cannot be careless -- about anything, large or small. The "stingless drones" reference comes from Hesiod (Works and Days 304) and is also used by Plato in Republic VIII (552c) and IX (564b) to describe parasitic members of society. The principle that god cannot possess a nature god would hate is a theological axiom with enormous consequences: it rules out any attribution of vice to the divine, more firmly even than Republic II (379a-380c). The either/or that follows (10.33) will show that both possible explanations for divine neglect lead to absurdity. Cross-reference: the argument from divine perfection to divine care mirrors Leibniz's later argument that a perfect God must create the best of all possible worlds (Theodicy, 1710).

narrative:
"Either he must think that the neglect of the small matters is of no consequence to the whole, or if he knows that they are of consequence, and he neglects them, his neglect must be attributed to carelessness and indolence. Is there any other way in which his neglect can be explained? For surely, when it is impossible for him to take care of all, he is not negligent if he fails to attend to these things great or small, which a God or some inferior being might be wanting in strength or capacity to manage?" said the Athenian.

"Certainly not," agreed Cleinias.
refashioned:
"There are only two possible explanations," the Athenian said. "Either the god believes that neglecting small matters has no consequence for the whole -- or he knows that small matters are consequential but neglects them anyway out of carelessness and indolence. Is there any third possibility?

Of course, when someone genuinely lacks the strength or capacity to manage everything -- whether god or lesser being -- failing to attend to certain matters, great or small, is not negligence at all."

"Certainly not," Cleinias agreed.
elegant: Only two explanations exist for neglecting small things: either one thinks they do not matter to the whole, or one knows they matter but is too careless and lazy to attend to them. A third possibility -- genuine inability -- would excuse the neglect, but this cannot apply to an omnipotent god.
summary: The Athenian narrows the possible explanations for divine neglect to two: either the god thinks small matters are inconsequential to the whole, or the god knows they matter but neglects them through carelessness. Genuine inability to manage everything would be an excuse, but it cannot apply to an all-powerful god.
distilled: Divine neglect of small matters can only be explained by either thinking they do not matter or being too lazy to attend to them -- neither of which is compatible with a perfect, omnipotent god.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. This passage presents a dilemma that eliminates both possible explanations for divine neglect: (1) if god thinks small things do not matter, this is ignorance -- incompatible with omniscience; (2) if god knows they matter but neglects them, this is laziness -- incompatible with perfect virtue (established in 10.32). The third option (inability) is explicitly set aside as inapplicable to omnipotent beings. The logical structure is an exhaustive disjunction: the only possible explanations are all shown to be inconsistent with divine perfection. Cross-reference: this argument anticipates the structure of Epicurus's trilemma about evil (if god is willing but unable, unable but willing, or neither -- reported by Lactantius, De Ira Dei 13.19). The argument also connects to Republic I (352d-354a), where Socrates argues that the function of each thing requires its proper virtue -- neglect of function implies vice.

narrative: "Now, then, let us examine the offenders, who both alike confess that there are Gods, but with a difference -- the one saying that they may be appeased, and the other that they have no care of small matters: there are three of us and two of them, and we will say to them -- 'In the first place, you both acknowledge that the Gods hear and see and know all things, and that nothing can escape them which is matter of sense and knowledge: do you admit this?'" said the Athenian.
refashioned:
"Now let us address both remaining groups together," the Athenian said, "since both confess that gods exist but disagree about their nature -- one claiming gods can be bought off, the other that they ignore small matters. There are three of us and two of them.

We begin with a shared premise: 'You both acknowledge that the gods hear, see, and know all things -- that nothing escapes them which falls within the scope of sensation or knowledge. Do you admit this?'"
elegant: The Athenian now addresses both remaining groups -- those who say gods can be bribed and those who say gods ignore small matters -- with a common starting point: both admit that gods hear, see, and know all things. Nothing escapes divine awareness.
summary: The Athenian begins to address the second and third forms of impiety simultaneously, since both groups acknowledge the gods' existence. He establishes a shared premise: both parties admit that the gods are omniscient -- they hear, see, and know all things, with nothing escaping their awareness.
distilled: Both those who claim gods can be bribed and those who claim gods are negligent share a premise: the gods hear, see, and know everything -- nothing escapes their awareness.
notes: Speaker: Athenian Stranger. The strategic decision to address both remaining forms of impiety together (divine negligence and divine corruptibility) is efficient: both share the premise of divine omniscience, which will be used against both positions. The three-versus-two framing (three interlocutors versus two impious positions) gives the scene a quasi-judicial character, appropriate for a legislative context. The premise of divine omniscience is presented as non-controversial -- even the impious concede it -- and will serve as the foundation for arguments against both negligence (if gods know everything, they cannot be ignorant of small matters) and corruptibility (if gods know everything, they cannot be deceived by bribes). Cross-reference: Republic II (381b-c) argues that gods, being perfectly wise, cannot be deceived. The omniscience premise also connects to the Timaeus (34a-b), where the World Soul knows all things through its pervasive contact with the cosmos.

narrative:
"Yes," said Cleinias.

"And do you admit also that they have all power which mortals and immortals can have?" asked the Athenian.

"They will, of course, admit this also," said Cleinias.

"And surely we three and they two -- five in all -- have acknowledged that they are good and perfect?" said the Athenian.

"Assuredly," agreed Cleinias.

"But, if they are such as we conceive them to be, can we possibly suppose that they ever act in the spirit of carelessness and indolence? For in us inactivity is the child of cowardice, and carelessness of inactivity and indolence," said the Athenian.

"Most true," said Cleinias.

"Then not from inactivity and carelessness is any God ever negligent; for there is no cowardice in them," said the Athenian.

"That is very true," agreed Cleinias.

"Then the alternative which remains is, that if the Gods neglect the lighter and lesser concerns of the universe, they neglect them because they know that they ought not to care about such matters -- what other alternative is there but the opposite of their knowing?" asked the Athenian.

"There is none," said Cleinias.

"And, O most excellent and best of men, do I understand you to mean that they are careless because they are ignorant, and do not know that they ought to take care, or that they know, and yet like the meanest sort of men, knowing the better, choose the worse because they are overcome by pleasures and pains?" asked the Athenian.

"Impossible," said Cleinias.

"Do not all human things partake of the nature of soul? And is not man the most religious of all animals?" asked the Athenian.

"That is not to be denied," agreed Cleinias.

"And we acknowledge that all mortal creatures are the property of the Gods, to whom also the whole of heaven belongs?" asked the Athenian.

"Certainly," said Cleinias.

"And, therefore, whether a person says that these things are to the Gods great or small -- in either case it would not be natural for the Gods who own us, and who are the most careful and the best of owners, to neglect us. There is also a further consideration," said the Athenian.

"What is it?" asked Cleinias.

"Sensation and power are in an inverse ratio to each other in respect to their ease and difficulty," said the Athenian.

"What do you mean?" asked Cleinias.

"I mean that there is greater difficulty in seeing and hearing the small than the great, but more facility in moving and controlling and taking care of small and unimportant things than of their opposites," said the Athenian.

"Far more," agreed Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Yes," Cleinias confirmed.

"And do they also have all the power that mortals and immortals can possess?"

"They will certainly admit that too."

"So all five of us -- we three and the two impious positions -- agree that the gods are good and perfect?"

"Without question."

"Then can we suppose that perfect beings ever act from carelessness or indolence? In human beings, inactivity comes from cowardice, and carelessness from inactivity and laziness."

"That is certainly true."

"But there is no cowardice in the gods -- so divine negligence cannot come from carelessness or inactivity."

"Agreed."

"The only remaining possibility is that the gods neglect lesser things because they believe such things do not require their attention. But the only alternative to knowing this is not knowing it."

"That is so."

"Then are you saying the gods are careless because they are ignorant of what deserves their attention? Or that they know what is right but, like the worst of men, choose the worse over the better because they are overcome by pleasures and pains?"

"Impossible -- neither could be true."

"Consider further: all human things partake of soul's nature, and man is the most religious of all creatures?"

"Undeniably."

"And all mortal creatures belong to the gods, as does the whole of heaven?"

"Yes."

"Then whether you call human affairs great or small relative to the gods, it would be unnatural for the gods -- who own us and are the most careful and excellent of owners -- to neglect us. And there is a further point."

"What?"

"Perception and power stand in an inverse ratio regarding difficulty. It is harder to perceive small things than large ones, but easier to control and care for small things than large ones."

"Far easier."
elegant: The gods are omniscient, omnipotent, and perfectly good. Negligence cannot come from cowardice (which gods lack), ignorance (which contradicts omniscience), or weakness of will (which contradicts perfect virtue). As the owners of all mortal creatures, the gods are the most careful of owners. And a further point: while small things are harder to perceive, they are easier to control. Since the gods can perceive everything, the care of small matters is the easiest task of all for them.
summary: The Athenian builds a comprehensive case against divine negligence. The gods are omniscient, omnipotent, and perfectly virtuous -- so negligence cannot stem from cowardice, ignorance, or weakness of will. As owners of all creation, they would naturally care for their property. And since perception and power are inversely related to scale (small things are hard to see but easy to manage), divine omniscience makes the care of small matters trivially easy.
distilled: The gods' omniscience, omnipotence, and perfect virtue make negligence impossible: they can perceive all things and manage small matters with the greatest ease, having no cowardice, ignorance, or weakness to prevent them.
notes: Speakers: Cleinias, Athenian Stranger. This passage assembles four arguments against divine negligence: (1) the argument from virtue -- gods cannot be lazy or cowardly; (2) the argument from knowledge -- gods cannot be ignorant of what needs care; (3) the argument from ownership -- gods, as the best of owners, would naturally care for their property (humans); (4) the argument from inverse difficulty -- small things are easier to manage even if harder to perceive, and gods perceive everything. The ownership argument is distinctive: mortals are "property of the gods" (ktema theon), a concept with both religious and legal resonance in Greek thought. The inverse-ratio argument is original and clever: it dissolves the intuition that small things are beneath divine notice by distinguishing perception difficulty from management difficulty. Cross-reference: the argument that the good person cares about details connects to Republic IV (421c-422e) on the guardian's comprehensive attention to the city. The ownership metaphor connects to Laws V (726a) on the soul belonging to the gods.

narrative:
"Suppose the case of a physician who is willing and able to cure some living thing as a whole -- how will the whole fare at his hands if he takes care only of the greater and neglects the parts which are lesser?" asked the Athenian.

"Decidedly not well," said Cleinias.

"No better would be the result with pilots or generals, or householders or statesmen, or any other such class, if they neglected the small and regarded only the great; as the builders say, the larger stones do not lie well without the lesser," said the Athenian.

"Of course not," agreed Cleinias.

"Let us not, then, deem God inferior to human workmen, who, in proportion to their skill, finish and perfect their works, small as well as great, by one and the same art; or that God, the wisest of beings, who is both willing and able to take care, is like a lazy good-for-nothing, or a coward, who turns his back upon labour and gives no thought to smaller and easier matters, but to the greater only," said the Athenian.

"Never, Stranger, let us admit a supposition about the Gods which is both impious and false," said Cleinias.

"I think that we have now argued enough with him who delights to accuse the Gods of neglect," said the Athenian.

"Yes," agreed Cleinias.

"He has been forced to acknowledge that he is in error, but he still seems to me to need some words of consolation," said the Athenian.

"What consolation will you offer him?" asked Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Consider a physician, willing and able to heal a creature as a whole," the Athenian said. "How will the whole fare if he attends only to the major organs and neglects the smaller parts?"

"Poorly."

"The same goes for pilots, generals, householders, and statesmen. If they attend only to great matters while neglecting small ones, the results are disastrous. As builders say: the large stones do not sit well without the small ones."

"Of course not."

"Then let us not suppose that god is inferior to human craftsmen, who -- in proportion to their skill -- finish and perfect works both small and great through one and the same art. Shall we say that god, the wisest of all beings, willing and able to take care, is like a lazy good-for-nothing or a coward who turns his back on work and ignores smaller, easier matters while attending only to the greater?"

"Never, Stranger -- let us not entertain a supposition about the gods that is both impious and false."

"I think we have argued enough with the person who delights in accusing the gods of neglect."

"Agreed."

"He has been compelled to acknowledge his error. But he still seems to need some consolation."

"What consolation will you offer?"
elegant: A physician who neglects small ailments harms the whole patient. A builder who ignores small stones weakens the whole structure. Every skilled craftsman perfects both small and great through the same art. God, the wisest of all, cannot be less competent or less diligent than a human workman. The case against divine negligence is closed -- but the defeated interlocutor deserves some consolation.
summary: The Athenian clinches the argument against divine negligence with analogies: physicians, pilots, generals, builders, and craftsmen all fail when they neglect small things. God cannot be deemed inferior to human artisans who attend to details. The argument against divine negligence is declared complete, though the Athenian promises consoling words to the defeated position.
distilled: Just as no skilled physician, builder, or craftsman can perfect the whole while neglecting the parts, god -- the wisest being -- cannot neglect small matters without contradicting divine excellence.
notes: Speakers: Athenian Stranger, Cleinias. The argument from analogy is characteristically Platonic: the craftsman (demiourgos) serves as the model for divine activity, just as in the Timaeus the divine craftsman creates the cosmos (Timaeus 28a-29a). The builder's proverb -- "the larger stones do not lie well without the lesser" -- is drawn from practical wisdom and grounding the philosophical argument in everyday experience. The sequence of analogies (physician, pilot, general, householder, statesman, builder) covers the full range of human expertise, making the point universal. The transition to "consolation" is characteristic of the Laws' pastoral tone -- the goal is not merely to defeat the impious but to heal them. Cross-reference: Republic I (341c-342e) uses the physician analogy to argue that true expertise serves the interest of its object, not the practitioner. Statesman (259a-260b) develops the analogy between divine and human governance. The builders' proverb has parallels in Hippocratic medical texts on the interdependence of body parts.

narrative:
"Let us say to the youth: 'The ruler of the universe has ordered all things with a view to the excellence and preservation of the whole, and each part, as far as may be, has an action and passion appropriate to it. Over these, down to the least fraction of them, ministers have been appointed to preside, who have wrought out their perfection with infinitesimal exactness. And one of these portions of the universe is thine own, unhappy man, which, however little, contributes to the whole; and you do not seem to be aware that this and every other creation is for the sake of the whole, and in order that the life of the whole may be blessed; and that you are created for the sake of the whole, and not the whole for the sake of you. For every physician and every skilled artist does all things for the sake of the whole, directing his effort towards the common good, executing the part for the sake of the whole, and not the whole for the sake of the part. And you are annoyed because you are ignorant how what is best for you happens to you and to the universe, as far as the laws of the common creation admit. Now, as the soul combining first with one body and then with another undergoes all sorts of changes, either of herself, or through the influence of another soul, all that remains to the player of the game is that he should shift the pieces; sending the better nature to the better place, and the worse to the worse, and so assigning to them their proper portion,'" said the Athenian.

"In what way do you mean?" asked Cleinias.
refashioned:
"Let us say to the young man: 'The ruler of the universe has arranged all things for the excellence and preservation of the whole. Every part, down to the smallest fraction, has been assigned an appropriate role. Over each part, ministers have been appointed who carry out their work with infinitesimal precision.

You, unhappy man, are one small portion of this universe -- yet even your small part contributes to the whole. What you fail to grasp is that every created thing exists for the sake of the whole, so that the life of the whole may be blessed. You are created for the sake of the whole -- not the whole for the sake of you.

Every physician, every skilled artisan, works for the sake of the whole, directing effort toward the common good, executing the part for the sake of the whole rather than the whole for the sake of the part. You are frustrated only because you do not understand how what is best for you coincides with what is best for the universe, within the constraints of universal law.

As the soul passes from one body to another, undergoing every kind of change -- whether through its own action or the influence of other souls -- all that remains for the divine player of the game is to move the pieces: sending the better nature to the better place, and the worse to the worse, assigning each its proper portion.'"

"What do you mean by that?" asked Cleinias.
